chunks.title: Carbon Dioxide Levels and Sleep Quality
chunks.summary: The speakers delve into the significance of carbon dioxide levels in the bedroom, explaining how increased levels can negatively affect sleep quality. They suggest simple solutions to maintain lower CO2 levels during sleep.
chunks.transcript: What are you doing now to optimize sleep? >> So, it depends where I am, right? But um the one of the more interesting things that I've been thinking about with sleep recently is this guy. So this is just I got this on Amazon. Uh it's a CO2 meter, right? So you can see that right now in this room it's 677 parts per million. And we've been recording in here for a little bit and when we started it was I think 540. >> So we've only been in here for 45 minutes an hour and it's already gone up more than 100 parts per million. But what I've when I've looked at the research, the meaningful cut off point for this with regard to sleep is probably 900 parts per million give or take. 8 850 to 900 parts per million. So ambient carbon dioxide, if we're outside, we were just working out in your gym here and if we have the door open, it'll be like 450 410 to 450 parts per million carbon dioxide. What's so interesting for me, and I didn't really understand this till I looked at this, is that every hour at rest, humans produce 15 lers of carbon dioxide. If you're exerting yourself, it can be 40 to 100 lers of carbon dioxide per hour. So, you think if you are in a you know, maybe it's a I don't know how big your bedroom, say your bedroom's 20 by 20, that's a huge bedroom, right? And you're in there with your wife and your dog. Over the course of eight to nine hours, if the whole room is closed, you are talking about hundreds of liters of carbon dioxide that go into that room that are not getting c where are they going? Right? And maybe the air conditioning is pulling them out, but it's all the whole house is just sort of circulating this air. So, a lot of people if they're in a small room especially will see or multiple people are sleeping in the same room plus animals, you'll see levels of carbon dioxide that easily go over a thousand parts per million while you're sleeping. That's wild. and up to 2,000 even. There's clear data that once you pass 8 to 900 parts per million, it affects sleep quality. It increases the amount of times you wake up per hour. It decreases sleep efficiency and it decreases the way people feel when they wake up. So, like you really want to keep your bedroom levels of carbon dioxide below 900 parts per million while you're sleeping. And it's so simple. It's like open a window. I mean, this is like a $40 thing on Amazon, but it's been so interesting for me to use it. And you I mean especially if you're in a house also so maybe you live in Los Angeles you keep everything closed all day. I mean when I go home to Virginia my parents it's humid in the summer they just they never open a window. My parents don't even understand what opening a window is about. They everyone is trying to keep the air conditioning in. You don't want to pay a big AC bill. But what is the carbon dioxide in your house in a day? If it's a small house or a studio it could easily get over a thousand. So you could be sitting in a thousand parts per million all day working and then you go to sleep and it could go over 2,000 and that's definitely going to affect sleep quality

chunks.title: Optimizing Noise and Light for Better Sleep
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to strategies for minimizing noise and light when sleeping. Tips include using silicone earplugs, covering windows with garbage bags, and the need for complete darkness to improve sleep quality.

chunks.title: Air Quality and Room Environment
chunks.summary: The speakers emphasize the importance of air quality and proper ventilation for a good night's sleep. They mention tactics for ensuring airflow while maintaining darkness in the room for optimal sleep conditions.

chunks.title: Managing Sleep Schedule
chunks.summary: The discussion concludes with insights on the significance of maintaining a consistent sleep schedule, highlighting the challenges of adapting to different time zones while prioritizing sleep hygiene.
